payload.formula_text:
First-Touch Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) from USER_ACQUISITION (first visit attribution)
Last-Touch Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) from TRAFFIC_ACQUISITION (session-level attribution)

First-Touch shows: Revenue credited to the channel that first brought the user
Last-Touch shows: Revenue credited to the channel of the converting session
payload.formula_explanation: Compares two attribution models: First-Touch (credits the channel that first acquired the user) vs Last-Touch (credits the channel of the converting session). Reveals how channels play different roles in awareness vs conversion.
how_to_read: Blue bars show First-Touch attribution (user acquisition)
Green bars show Last-Touch attribution (conversion session)
Channels with higher First-Touch drive awareness and discovery
Channels with higher Last-Touch close sales and drive conversions
Similar bars indicate the channel works across the full funnel
Compare bars to understand each channel role in the journey
decisions_answered: Which channels drive initial awareness vs final conversion?
Are we undervaluing awareness channels with last-touch attribution?
How do channels work together in the customer journey?
Should we adjust budget based on full-funnel contribution?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares First-Touch and Last-Touch attribution models - revealing how channels play different roles across the customer journey.

First-Touch credits revenue to the channel that first brought the user to your site. Last-Touch credits the channel of the actual converting session. These often differ significantly.

Reading this chart: Blue bars show First-Touch, green shows Last-Touch. Channels with taller blue bars drive awareness - they bring new users who may convert later through other channels. Taller green bars indicate closing power.

Paid Social often shows higher First-Touch than Last-Touch - it generates awareness but users convert through Direct or Search later. Email often shows stronger Last-Touch as it closes existing customers.

Key questions: Which channels drive awareness vs conversion? Are we undervaluing upper-funnel channels? How should we allocate budget considering full-funnel impact?

payload.formula_text:
Campaign Sessions = SUM(SESSIONS) GROUP BY SESSION_CAMPAIGN_NAME
Campaign Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) per campaign
Conversion Rate = (KEY_EVENTS / SESSIONS) x 100
Engagement Rate = AVG(ENGAGEMENT_RATE) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks individual campaign performance within customer journeys. Shows how specific marketing campaigns contribute to sessions, revenue, and conversions, enabling campaign-level optimization and budget decisions.
how_to_read: Each row represents a marketing campaign with full metrics
Sort by Revenue to find highest-performing campaigns
High Engagement Rate suggests relevant, quality traffic
Low Conversion Rate with high sessions needs landing page review
Compare Rev/Session to understand campaign efficiency
Use search to find specific campaigns by name
decisions_answered: Which campaigns drive the most revenue?
What is the conversion rate by campaign?
Which campaigns have the best engagement metrics?
Where should we increase or decrease campaign spend?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows individual campaign performance in customer journeys - essential for optimizing your marketing spend at the campaign level.

The formula aggregates sessions, revenue, conversions, and engagement for each named campaign from GA4. This includes UTM-tagged campaigns and auto-detected advertising campaigns.

Reading this table: Sort by Revenue to see top performers. Look at Conversion Rate alongside Revenue - high revenue with low conversion might indicate volume potential with optimization opportunity.

Engagement Rate shows traffic quality. High engagement with low conversion suggests the audience is interested but something in the funnel is blocking them. Low engagement indicates poor targeting or ad-to-landing-page misalignment.

Key questions: Which campaigns perform best? Where are conversion opportunities? What campaigns should scale or pause? Use the search to filter for specific campaigns.

payload.formula_text:
Channel Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) GROUP BY SESSION_DEFAULT_CHANNEL_GROUPING
Sessions = SUM(SESSIONS) per channel
Revenue Per Session = Total Revenue / Sessions
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates session-level revenue attribution from GA4 by default channel grouping. Shows which marketing channels drive the most revenue, enabling budget allocation decisions across paid, organic, and owned channels.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show revenue contribution from each channel
Longer bars indicate higher revenue attribution to that channel
Direct and Organic Search typically lead for established brands
Compare paid channels (Paid Search, Paid Social) to organic performance
Hover to see sessions, conversions, and revenue per session metrics
Click any channel to filter the entire dashboard by that channel
decisions_answered: Which marketing channels drive the most revenue?
How does paid channel performance compare to organic channels?
Is our Email marketing driving proportional revenue?
Which channels offer the best ROI for additional investment?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart shows revenue attribution by marketing channel - essential for understanding where your customers come from and what drives purchases.

The formula sums total revenue for each GA4 default channel grouping over the selected date range, then calculates supporting metrics like sessions and revenue per session.

Reading this chart: Longer bars mean more revenue from that channel. Direct and Organic Search often lead for established brands with strong brand awareness. Paid channels show your advertising impact.

Compare your paid channels to organic. High organic revenue means strong brand equity. High paid means your advertising is working but you may be more acquisition-dependent.

Key questions: Which channels drive most revenue? How do paid vs organic compare? Where should you invest more? Click any channel to focus the entire page on that traffic source.

payload.formula_text:
Daily Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) GROUP BY DATE, CHANNEL
Stacked Total = Running SUM across channels per day
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks daily revenue contribution from each major channel as a stacked area chart. Shows how channel mix evolves over time and reveals seasonal patterns, campaign impacts, and channel interdependencies.
how_to_read: Each colored area represents one marketing channel stacked on others
The total height shows combined daily revenue across all channels
Watch for channel proportions changing over time
Spikes may indicate successful campaigns or seasonal events
Consistent patterns suggest stable channel performance
Click to isolate individual channel series for closer analysis
decisions_answered: How is our channel mix evolving over time?
Are any channels growing or declining in contribution?
What impact do campaigns have on channel performance?
Are there seasonal patterns in channel effectiveness?
payload.narration_text:
This stacked area chart shows how channel revenue contributions evolve over time - revealing trends, patterns, and campaign impacts.

The formula sums daily revenue for each channel, then stacks them to show both individual channel performance and total revenue trajectory.

Reading this chart: Each colored layer represents a channel. The total height shows combined revenue. Watch how layer proportions shift over time - growing layers indicate channels gaining importance.

Look for spikes that coincide with campaigns. A spike in Paid Search might show a Google Ads campaign working. A jump in Email indicates a successful send. Dips might signal issues needing investigation.

Key questions: How is channel mix evolving? Are channels growing or shrinking? What patterns exist? Click any channel in the legend to isolate its trend line.

payload.formula_text:
Signup Month = DATE_TRUNC('month', CREATED)
New Profiles = COUNT(*) per month
Avg CLV = AVG(PREDICTIVE_ANALYTICS_TOTAL_CLV) per cohort
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks average predicted customer lifetime value by the month customers were acquired. Shows whether customer quality is improving or declining over time, helping evaluate acquisition strategy effectiveness.
how_to_read: Each point represents customers acquired in that month
The Y-axis shows average predicted lifetime value
Rising line indicates improving customer quality over time
Declining line suggests acquisition strategy may be degrading
Seasonal dips may reflect promotional acquisition periods
Hover to see the cohort size and exact CLV value
decisions_answered: Is our customer quality improving or declining?
Which acquisition months brought the highest-value customers?
Are recent cohorts more or less valuable than historical ones?
Should we adjust acquisition strategy to improve CLV?
payload.narration_text:
This line chart tracks average customer lifetime value by acquisition cohort - showing whether your customer quality is improving over time.

The formula groups customers by the month they were created in Klaviyo, then calculates the average predicted CLV for each cohort from Klaviyo predictive analytics.

Reading this chart: Each point represents a monthly cohort. The Y-axis shows their average lifetime value. An upward trend means you're acquiring higher-value customers over time - your strategy is improving.

Watch for seasonal patterns. Cohorts acquired during heavy promotional periods may show lower CLV due to discount-seeking customers. Organic growth periods often produce higher-value cohorts.

Key questions: Is customer quality improving? Which months produced the best customers? Are recent cohorts valuable? Click any point to analyze that specific acquisition cohort.

payload.formula_text:
Sessions = COUNT(session_start events)
View Item = COUNT(view_item events)
Add to Cart = COUNT(add_to_cart events)
Begin Checkout = COUNT(begin_checkout events)
Purchase = COUNT(purchase events)

Conversion Rate = (Current Stage / Previous Stage) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates GA4 e-commerce funnel events to show customer progression from initial session through final purchase. Each stage shows the absolute count and the conversion rate to the next stage, helping identify where customers drop off.
how_to_read: The funnel flows left to right showing customer journey stages
Bar width represents volume at each stage relative to sessions
Percentages show conversion rate from the previous stage
Drop-off indicators highlight where customers leave the funnel
Red drop-off arrows show percentage lost between stages
Click any stage to drill into detailed analysis for that step
decisions_answered: What is the overall conversion rate from session to purchase?
At which funnel stage do we lose the most customers?
Is cart abandonment or checkout abandonment our bigger problem?
How does our funnel conversion compare to industry benchmarks?
payload.narration_text:
This funnel visualization shows your customer journey from initial session through final purchase - the core of conversion optimization.

The formula counts each funnel event type from GA4: session starts, product views, cart additions, checkout initiations, and purchases. Conversion rates are calculated between adjacent stages.

Reading this chart: Follow the horizontal funnel from left to right. Each bar represents volume at that stage. The percentages between stages show your stage-to-stage conversion rates.

Watch for the biggest drops. Is it view-to-cart where product appeal matters? Cart-to-checkout where friction enters? Or checkout-to-purchase where trust and payment issues arise?

Key questions answered: What's our overall conversion rate? Where do we lose the most customers? What's the priority for optimization? Click any stage for deeper analysis into that conversion point.

payload.formula_text:
Device Users = SUM(TOTAL_USERS) GROUP BY DEVICE_CATEGORY
Device Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) per device
Engagement Rate = AVG(ENGAGEMENT_RATE)
Revenue Per User = Revenue / Users
payload.formula_explanation: Shows user distribution and performance across mobile, desktop, and tablet devices. Helps identify device-specific experience issues and opportunities for optimization by revealing where users engage but may not convert.
how_to_read: The donut shows proportional user distribution by device
Blue represents Mobile - typically the largest segment
Green represents Desktop - often highest conversion
Orange represents Tablet - usually smallest segment
Center shows total users across all devices
Hover to see revenue and engagement rate by device
decisions_answered: What is our device mix for website traffic?
Which devices show highest engagement and conversion?
Is our mobile experience converting as well as desktop?
Do we need device-specific UX optimizations?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how users distribute across mobile, desktop, and tablet devices - critical for understanding where to invest in UX optimization.

The formula sums users by device category from GA4, along with revenue and engagement metrics for each device type.

Reading this chart: Mobile typically dominates user volume - most browsing happens on phones. Desktop often shows higher conversion rates due to easier checkout. Tablet is usually the smallest segment.

The key insight is comparing device proportions for users vs revenue. If mobile is 70 percent of users but only 40 percent of revenue, there is a mobile conversion opportunity worth investigating.

Key questions: What's our device mix? Do conversion rates match user volume? Is mobile experience holding back revenue? Click any segment to analyze that device in detail.

payload.formula_text:
Metric Events = COUNT(EVENT.ID) GROUP BY METRIC.NAME
Unique Users = COUNT(DISTINCT PERSON_ID) per metric
Includes: Received Email, Opened Email, Clicked Email, Received SMS, Clicked SMS, Placed Order, Started Checkout, Added To Cart, Viewed Product
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks email and SMS marketing touchpoints from Klaviyo throughout the customer journey. Shows engagement levels at each stage and how marketing communications influence customer behavior.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show event volume for each Klaviyo metric
Email metrics include Received, Opened, and Clicked
SMS metrics include Received and Clicked
Commerce events show Viewed Product through Placed Order
Compare Opened to Received for open rate insights
Click any metric to analyze that touchpoint in detail
decisions_answered: How many emails and SMS messages are we sending?
What are our email open and click rates?
How do Klaviyo touchpoints influence purchases?
Is our email/SMS engagement improving over time?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows Klaviyo touchpoint metrics - measuring how your email and SMS marketing engages customers throughout their journey.

The formula counts events by metric type from Klaviyo: emails sent, opened, clicked, SMS messages, and commerce events like product views and purchases.

Reading this chart: The bars show event volume for each metric type. Compare Received Email to Opened Email to gauge open rates. Compare Opened to Clicked for click-through performance.

Commerce events like Placed Order show how many Klaviyo-tracked customers are completing purchases. These help attribute revenue back to your email and SMS marketing efforts.

Key questions: What's our email volume? How are open and click rates performing? What commerce activity ties to Klaviyo? Click any metric to dive into that specific touchpoint.

payload.formula_text:
Page Views = SUM(SCREEN_PAGE_VIEWS) GROUP BY PAGE_PATH
New Users = SUM(NEW_USERS) per page
Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) attributed to page
Avg Engagement = AVG(USER_ENGAGEMENT_DURATION) in seconds
payload.formula_explanation: Shows which landing pages receive the most traffic and drive the most value. Helps identify high-performing pages to replicate and underperforming pages that need optimization or elimination.
how_to_read: Each row shows a landing page with key performance metrics
Sort by Page Views to see highest traffic pages
Sort by Revenue to see pages driving the most value
High New Users indicates effective acquisition pages
Avg Engagement shows page quality and relevance
Use search to find specific pages or page categories
decisions_answered: Which landing pages receive the most traffic?
What pages drive the most revenue attribution?
Which pages have the best engagement metrics?
Where should we focus landing page optimization efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your top landing pages by traffic and performance - essential for understanding where customers enter your site and how well those entry points convert.

The formula aggregates page views, new users, revenue, and engagement time for each page path from GA4. This shows which pages serve as effective entry points.

Reading this table: Sort by Page Views for traffic volume, by Revenue for value generation. Home page often leads traffic, but product and category pages may drive more revenue per view.

Look for mismatches. A page with high traffic but low revenue may need conversion optimization. A page with great revenue per view but low traffic deserves more promotion.

Key questions: Which pages get most traffic? What drives most revenue? Where should optimization focus? Use the search to analyze specific page categories like product pages or blog posts.

payload.formula_text:
Order Bucket = CASE WHEN orders = 1 THEN '1 order'
                       WHEN orders = 2 THEN '2 orders'
                       WHEN orders BETWEEN 3 AND 5 THEN '3-5 orders'
                       WHEN orders BETWEEN 6 AND 10 THEN '6-10 orders'
                       WHEN orders > 10 THEN '10+ orders'
Customer Count = COUNT(*) per bucket
Avg CLV = AVG(PREDICTIVE_ANALYTICS_TOTAL_CLV) per bucket
payload.formula_explanation: Segments customers by their historical purchase frequency from Klaviyo predictive analytics. Shows the distribution of one-time vs repeat buyers and highlights the value of moving customers up the frequency ladder.
how_to_read: Each bar shows customer count at that purchase frequency
One-time buyers (1 order) typically form the largest group
Smaller bars to the right represent loyal repeat customers
The goal is to grow higher-frequency buckets over time
Hover to see average CLV for each frequency segment
Click any bucket to filter and analyze that customer segment
decisions_answered: What percentage of customers are one-time buyers?
How many customers have become loyal repeat purchasers?
What is the LTV difference between frequency segments?
Where should retention campaigns focus?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows customer distribution by purchase frequency - revealing your repeat purchase opportunity and customer loyalty levels.

The formula buckets customers by their historical order count from Klaviyo, grouping them into meaningful frequency segments from one-time buyers to loyal ten-plus order customers.

Reading this chart: The leftmost bar shows one-time buyers - typically the largest segment. Each bar to the right shows higher-frequency customers. The goal is to shift customers rightward over time.

Notice how customer count shrinks but value grows at higher frequencies. A customer with ten-plus orders might have ten times the LTV of a one-time buyer. Moving just a fraction of one-time buyers to two orders significantly impacts revenue.

Key questions: What's our one-time buyer percentage? How many loyal customers exist? What's the LTV at each frequency? Click any bucket to see those customers and plan targeted campaigns.

payload.formula_text:
Source/Medium Revenue = SUM(TOTAL_REVENUE) GROUP BY SESSION_SOURCE, SESSION_MEDIUM
Conversions = SUM(KEY_EVENTS)
Conv Rate = (Conversions / Sessions) x 100
Rev Per Session = Revenue / Sessions
payload.formula_explanation: Provides granular attribution at the source/medium level, more detailed than channel groupings. Shows specific traffic sources like google/cpc, facebook/paid, or newsletter/email to pinpoint exact acquisition drivers.
how_to_read: Each row shows a specific source/medium combination
Sort by Revenue to see top revenue drivers
Sort by Conversion Rate to find most efficient sources
Google/organic typically dominates for SEO-strong brands
Direct/(none) represents bookmarks and direct navigation
Click any row to filter and analyze that source in detail
decisions_answered: Which specific traffic sources drive the most revenue?
What is the conversion rate by source/medium?
Which sources are most efficient (high rev per session)?
Where should we focus optimization efforts?
payload.narration_text:
This table breaks down attribution to the source and medium level - more granular than channels for precise optimization decisions.

The formula groups revenue by the combination of source (where traffic came from) and medium (the type of traffic). This distinguishes google/cpc from google/organic, for example.

Reading this table: Sort by Revenue to see top performers. Sort by Conversion Rate to find efficiency. Google/organic often leads for brands with strong SEO. Direct represents loyal customers returning.

Look for sources with high conversion rates but lower volume - these may be scaling opportunities. Sources with high volume but low conversion may need landing page optimization.

Key questions: Which exact sources drive revenue? What are the conversion rates? Where are efficiency opportunities? Click any row to dive deeper into that source.
